metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add at least one acceptance criterion that replays a known failure-shape example (e.g., the Gerrit-password out-of-scope finding and the ticket-9e76 near-duplicate pair) through the modified T5c prompt in a test fixture or prompt-evaluation harness, asserting the new rules suppress the false positives while a known true-positive security finding is still emitted.
findings.finding: The proving commands for AC1 and AC2 verify only that specific text strings were inserted into the prompt file (structural grep checks), but no acceptance criterion exercises the modified T5c prompt behaviorally against the known failure cases cited in the evidence (e.g., the Gerrit-password out-of-scope finding, the temp-key-file near-duplicate pair). The plan's stated end-state is that T5c 'stops claiming out-of-scope assets and stops emitting near-duplicates while preserving recall on genuine gaps' — but the union of steps (add text → grep confirms → make test green) proves only that the text was added, not that the added rules produce the desired behavioral change. A prompt change that syntactically satisfies the grep oracle could still leave the LLM ignoring the new rules.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC1, AC2; plan Evidence section (failure shape examples)
findings.suggested_fix: Add an AC such as: 'A prompt-evaluation fixture (e.g., tests/reviewers/t5c_scope_dedup_test.py or equivalent) replays (a) the Gerrit-password out-of-scope scenario and asserts zero findings, (b) the ticket-9e76 near-duplicate scenario and asserts exactly one finding, and (c) a genuine in-scope secret-exposure scenario and asserts at least one finding. Oracle: `make test` runs and all three assertions pass.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC (or extend AC1/AC2) with a behavioral oracle that demonstrates recall is preserved: e.g., a fixture plan that explicitly creates/transmits a credential must still produce a T5c advisory after the new scope rule is applied (can be a golden-file or example-based test in the prompt test suite).
findings.finding: The plan's stated constraint 'without changing...recall on genuinely in-scope security gaps' has no end-state-observable proof. All four ACs verify text presence or routing stability; none verify that legitimate in-scope security findings are not inadvertently suppressed by the new scope rule. If the wording of the in-plan-asset rule is overly broad, recall could silently degrade with no AC catching it.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — all four ACs; plan goal statement ('without...changing...recall on genuinely in-scope security gaps')
findings.suggested_fix: Add an AC such as: 'Given a fixture plan that explicitly creates and transmits an API key, T5c still emits at least one advisory finding after the scope rule is applied. Oracle: the existing prompt-test harness (or a new fixture) exercises this case and passes under make test.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the value vocabulary for 'surface' and 'defect' in the dedup rule — specify whether 'surface' maps to a named asset/path/component from the plan and 'defect' maps to one of the T5c checklist keys (trust_boundary, access_classification, data_protection, least_privilege, secret_lifecycle), or provide an explicit taxonomy the T5c agent can apply mechanically.
findings.finding: The same-round dedup rule introduces '(surface, defect) pair' as the dedup key but neither 'surface' nor 'defect' is defined as an enum or given a value vocabulary anywhere in the plan or in the existing plan_review_T5c.md. The T5c agent implementing this rule cannot determine whether two findings share the same pair — is 'surface' an endpoint name, a credential path, a T5c sub-check key (e.g. secret_lifecycle), or a STRIDE category? Is 'defect' a free-form label or one of the T5c checklist keys? Without this vocabulary the rule is unenforceable.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria, AC2 ('one finding per (surface, defect) pair') / src/rebar/llm/reviewers/plan_review_T5c.md (proposed addition)
findings.suggested_fix: Rewrite the dedup rule as: 'One finding per (named asset/component, T5c sub-check key) pair — where the asset/component is the specific resource the plan names (e.g. an SSM parameter, a credential, an endpoint) and the sub-check key is one of: trust_boundary | access_classification | data_protection | least_privilege | secret_lifecycle. Additional angles on the same (asset, sub-check) pair are folded into one finding's text.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct AC3 to name the actual derived artifact (`docs/plan-review-criteria-guide.md`, regenerated via `python -m rebar.llm.plan_review.registry regenerate-criteria-guide`) and clarify that the drift gate for plan-review criteria is the `validate-routing` parity gate — not the `reviewers/index.json` prompt-index drift check, which is irrelevant to `category: plan-review-criterion` files.
findings.finding: AC3 refers to 're-generating the derived prompt index' and 'the prompt-index drift check', but plan_review_T5c.md has category: plan-review-criterion (not category: review), so it never contributes to reviewers/index.json or its regeneration. The actual derived artifact governed by the CI drift gate for plan-review criteria is docs/plan-review-criteria-guide.md, regenerated via `python -m rebar.llm.plan_review.registry regenerate-criteria-guide` and validated by validate_criteria_guide (folded into the validate-routing gate). This artifact is entirely unmentioned in the plan, leaving an implementer likely to target the wrong regeneration path or skip regeneration altogether.
findings.location: AC3 — 'The derived prompt index is regenerated if the build requires it and the CI prompt-drift gate passes.'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace AC3 with: 'Run `python -m rebar.llm.plan_review.registry regenerate-criteria-guide` to refresh `docs/plan-review-criteria-guide.md`; the CI parity gate (`python -m rebar.llm.plan_review.registry validate-routing`) must pass green. Oracle: `make lint && make test` green, with the validate-routing gate showing OK.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a co-located enumeration protocol to the in-plan-asset scope rule specifying: (1) how to build the in-scope asset set from the plan (scan named assets in steps that use create/modify/delete/transmit verbs), (2) what 'transmits' includes (e.g., passing a value to a network call counts; reading-only does not), and (3) the mechanical gate: 'if the finding's asset is not in the in-scope set, suppress the finding.'
findings.finding: The in-plan-asset scope rule specifies that a finding must name 'the plan section/step that creates, modifies, or transmits the asset at issue,' but provides no co-located protocol for how the T5c agent enumerates which assets are 'touched' by the plan. The decision rule — what verbs or operations count as 'touching' an asset, and how to scan plan steps to build that set — is referenced implicitly but never specified.

findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a fallback rule for ambiguous (surface, defect) pair membership — e.g., 'when uncertain whether two findings share the same pair, emit them separately and annotate the potential overlap rather than silently folding.'
findings.finding: The dedup rule specifies the fold action for matched pairs but provides no fallback for the case where the T5c agent cannot determine with certainty whether two findings share the same (surface, defect) pair — i.e., the pair-membership is ambiguous. No tie-breaking or default behavior is defined.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an empirical validation step: create a small fixture (e.g., 3–5 representative plan snippets — at least one known out-of-scope credential case, one near-duplicate pair, and one genuine in-scope security gap) and verify the modified prompt suppresses the first two without dropping the third, before merging.
findings.finding: The plan adds two LLM prompt rules (in-plan-asset scope gate and same-round dedup) and asserts they will reduce false-positive findings without degrading recall on genuine in-scope security gaps, but includes no empirical validation step — the acceptance-criteria oracles only verify text presence (grep) and CI green, not behavioral correctness of the modified prompt.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — all four ACs; Evidence paragraph citing 288 findings / failure shapes
findings.suggested_fix: Add an AC: 'A prompt-behavior fixture covering the two documented failure shapes (out-of-scope credential finding, near-duplicate pair) and one known true-positive in-scope security gap is run against the modified prompt; out-of-scope and duplicate findings are absent; the true-positive finding is present. Oracle: fixture test in tests/llm/test_T5c_prompt_hygiene.py passes.' This can be a one-shot LLM call in the test suite with a deterministic seed or a recorded response, keeping it lightweight.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a behavioral validation AC (e.g. a calibration run against the two documented failure shapes — the Gerrit-password false positive and the ticket-9e76 duplicate pair — or reference an equivalent eval fixture) to verify the new rules change LLM output, not just prompt text; and either adopt the structural post-processing dedup alternative or document why prompt-text instruction is preferred.
findings.finding: The plan asserts that adding prompt-text instructions ('one finding per (surface, defect) pair' and the in-plan-asset scope rule) will reliably suppress the observed failure shapes (288 advisory findings, duplicate pairs), but offers no calibration or eval evidence for the new rules' behavioral efficacy. Prompt-text dedup/scope instructions are a soft control that relies on LLM compliance; the existing test oracle (`make test`) only validates structural drift, not behavioral change. An alternative — a post-processing structural dedup of findings by (surface, defect) key before emitting — would enforce the rule mechanically without relying on LLM compliance, and its absence is not acknowledged or justified.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC1 (in-plan-asset scope rule) and AC2 (same-round dedup rule); Evidence section ('288 advisory findings in the last 7 days')
findings.suggested_fix: Add an AC such as: 'A calibration run against the two documented failure shapes (out-of-scope Gerrit-password finding; ticket-9e76 near-duplicate pair) shows each is suppressed under the new prompt. Oracle: a live eval or a recorded fixture in docs/calibration/T5c_prompt_hygiene.md.' This mirrors ADR 0018's calibration requirement for the prior T5c change.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a brief positive rationale statement to the plan explaining why adding prose rules directly to the T5c prompt is the right intervention layer (e.g., lowest blast-radius, consistent with all sibling rubrics, advisory posture makes prompt-level dedup sufficient vs. pipeline-level enforcement).
findings.finding: The plan does not state a positive rationale for choosing prompt-text rules as the mechanism (over alternatives like pipeline-level dedup filtering or structured finding schema enforcement). The Evidence section explains the problem and the division-of-labor boundary with ADR 0018, but does not explicitly say why inline prompt prose is the right layer for these two rules.
findings.location: Plan body / Evidence section (no dedicated rationale statement)
findings.suggested_fix: Add one sentence to the plan body such as: 'Prompt-text rules are the right layer here: all T5c behavior is driven by the rubric markdown (consistent with every sibling overlay), the advisory posture at 0.95 makes LLM-level dedup sufficient without requiring pipeline code changes, and the blast radius is bounded to a single file.'

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
